issues.title: No global error boundary fallback UI — crashes show blank screen
issues.description: The ErrorBoundary wraps the app but the code provides no visible fallback prop or fallback UI component. If any lazy-loaded page throws during render, users see a blank white screen with no recovery path. This violates the Global Acceptance Standard requiring inline retry and never a full-page blank error.
issues.prompt:
In the file src/App.tsx, make these specific changes: Find the component wrapping the app content. BEFORE: is used without a fallback prop, so render errors produce a blank screen with no user-facing message or recovery action. AFTER: Add a fallback prop to that renders a styled error card with: (1) a construction-themed icon, (2) the heading 'Something went wrong', (3) a brief message 'An unexpected error occurred. Please try again.', (4) a 'Reload Page' button that calls window.location.reload(), and (5) a 'Go to Dashboard' link that navigates to '/'. Use inline styles matching the design system: white card, 12px border-radius, 24px padding, centered on page with max-width 480px. The fallback should be a React component defined above the App function, e.g. function ErrorFallback() { return
...
}, then pass it as }>. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: Suspense fallback is a raw Skeleton with no aria-live announcement
issues.description: The Suspense boundary uses <Skeleton /> which has no aria-busy or aria-live region. Screen reader users get no feedback that content is loading. Every page navigation via lazy loading silently blanks for them. Violates WCAG 2.1 AA and Global Acceptance Standards for loading states.
issues.prompt:
In the file src/App.tsx, make these specific changes: Find all wrappers used for lazy-loaded page routes. BEFORE: The fallback is or a bare loading indicator with no ARIA attributes, so screen readers announce nothing during page loads. AFTER: Replace the Suspense fallback with a wrapper div that includes role='status' and aria-live='polite' and aria-label='Loading page content'. Inside, render the existing Skeleton component plus a visually-hidden span (using style={{ position: 'absolute', width: '1px', height: '1px', overflow: 'hidden', clip: 'rect(0,0,0,0)' }}) containing the text 'Loading page content'. Create a small component like: function PageLoadingFallback() { return (
Loading page content
); } Then use }>. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: ProtectedRoute may not redirect unauthenticated users to /login with returnTo
issues.description: The PAGE_ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA requires that unauthenticated users hitting any protected route get redirected to /login with a returnTo query parameter preserving the original URL. The current ProtectedRoute import exists but based on the auth spec pattern, it likely redirects to /login without returnTo, breaking the AUTH-003 test case and the deep-link integrity requirement.
issues.prompt: In the file src/components/auth/ProtectedRoute.tsx, make these specific changes in the ProtectedRoute component: BEFORE: When the user is not authenticated, the component redirects to '/login' (or renders <Navigate to='/login' />) without preserving the current URL the user was trying to access. AFTER: Import useLocation from react-router-dom. Inside the component, call const location = useLocation(). When the user is not authenticated (no user/session), render <Navigate to={`/login?returnTo=${encodeURIComponent(location.pathname + location.search)}`} replace /> instead of a plain <Navigate to='/login' />. Also ensure that during the loading state (while auth is being checked), the component renders a loading indicator (a div with role='status' containing 'Verifying access...') rather than briefly flashing the login page or the protected content. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: No viewport meta tag enforcement or mobile bottom nav routing in App shell
issues.description: The App shell renders Sidebar for desktop and MobileLayout for mobile, but the routing structure does not clearly handle the mobile breakpoint transition for bottom navigation. The MobileLayout component is imported but the conditional rendering logic between Sidebar and MobileLayout may cause layout thrashing or show both on tablet widths. The design standard requires bottom navigation on mobile, not hamburger menu.
issues.prompt:
In the file src/App.tsx, make these specific changes: Find where Sidebar and MobileLayout are rendered conditionally based on screen size. BEFORE: Both Sidebar and MobileLayout may render simultaneously or the breakpoint logic is unclear, causing layout conflicts on tablet (768-1024px) widths. The isMobile check from useMediaQuery may not have a clean cutoff. AFTER: Ensure the rendering is strictly exclusive: use the existing useMediaQuery hook to get isMobile (max-width 768px). Render MobileLayout (which should include bottom navigation) ONLY when isMobile is true. Render Sidebar ONLY when isMobile is false. Wrap this in a single conditional: {isMobile ? ...routes... :
...routes...
}. If MobileLayout is currently just a wrapper, ensure it does NOT render the Sidebar component inside it. This prevents double navigation on any screen size. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: API v1 router has duplicate file content and missing pathMatches export
issues.description: The router.ts file content appears duplicated in the codebase context — the entire file is repeated twice. Additionally the resolveRequiredScope function and pathMatches helper are defined but pathMatches is not exported, and the function's return type uses a union of null | undefined which is confusing. The first copy is truncated missing the closing brace of resolveRequiredScope.
issues.prompt: In the file src/api/v1/router.ts, make these specific changes: BEFORE: The file has a truncated resolveRequiredScope function (missing closing brace and the pathMatches helper in the first occurrence), and the return type is 'ApiKeyScope | null | undefined' which conflates 'route found but no scope needed' (null) with 'route not found' (undefined) in an ambiguous way. AFTER: Ensure the file contains exactly ONE copy of all code. Keep the complete resolveRequiredScope function with its pathMatches helper. Change the return type to be explicit: create a type alias 'type ScopeResult = { found: true; scope: ApiKeyScope | null } | { found: false }' and return that instead, OR simply add a JSDoc comment clarifying that undefined means 'no matching route' and null means 'session auth only, no API key scope required'. Also export pathMatches as a named export so it can be unit tested. Ensure no duplicate function definitions exist in the file. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: Lazy import failures silently crash with no retry mechanism
issues.description: All 25+ page components use React.lazy() with bare dynamic imports. If a chunk fails to load (common on construction site networks), the Suspense boundary catches the error but there is no retry mechanism. The user sees a blank screen or the ErrorBoundary fallback with no way to retry just the failed chunk load. Network-flaky field conditions make this a frequent real-world failure.
issues.prompt: In the file src/App.tsx, make these specific changes: BEFORE: All lazy imports use the pattern `lazy(() => import('./pages/X').then(m => ({ default: m.X })))` with no error recovery. A failed network request for a chunk permanently breaks that route until full page reload. AFTER: Create a helper function at the top of the file called `lazyWithRetry` that wraps the dynamic import with retry logic: `function lazyWithRetry(importFn: () => Promise<any>, retries = 3, delay = 1000) { return lazy(() => new Promise((resolve, reject) => { function attempt(retriesLeft: number) { importFn().then(resolve).catch((err: Error) => { if (retriesLeft > 0) { setTimeout(() => attempt(retriesLeft - 1), delay); } else { reject(err); } }); } attempt(retries); })); }`. Then replace the first 5 most critical page lazy imports (Dashboard, RFIs, DailyLog, PunchList, Schedule) to use `lazyWithRetry(() => import('./pages/Dashboard').then(m => ({ default: m.Dashboard })))` instead of bare `lazy()`. This ensures field users on flaky connections get automatic retries before seeing an error. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: All 25+ pages loaded as separate lazy chunks without route-based grouping
issues.description: Every single page is a separate lazy chunk. For construction PMs who navigate between RFIs, Submittals, and Change Orders frequently, this means 3 separate chunk downloads. Grouping related pages into bundles would reduce waterfall network requests.
issues.prompt: In the file src/App.tsx, make these specific changes: BEFORE: Each page like RFIs, Submittals, ChangeOrders is a separate lazy import creating individual chunks. AFTER: No code change needed for this cycle — this is a minor optimization. Document with a TODO comment above the lazy imports: `// TODO: Consider grouping related pages (RFIs + Submittals + ChangeOrders) into a single chunk using webpackChunkName or Vite manual chunks for frequently co-visited pages`. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: useRealtimeInvalidation hook imported but subscription scope unclear
issues.description: The useRealtimeInvalidation hook is imported and presumably called in the App shell, but without a project_id filter it may subscribe to all changes across all projects, wasting bandwidth and causing unnecessary re-renders. The hook should be scoped to the active project.
issues.prompt: In the file src/App.tsx, make these specific changes: Find where useRealtimeInvalidation() is called. BEFORE: The hook may be called without a project ID argument, subscribing to all realtime changes globally. AFTER: Ensure useRealtimeInvalidation is called with the current project ID from useProjectId(). If useProjectId returns null (no project selected), the hook should be a no-op (do not subscribe). The call should look like: `const projectId = useProjectId(); useRealtimeInvalidation(projectId ?? undefined);`. If the hook does not accept a projectId parameter, add a TODO comment: `// TODO: Scope realtime subscriptions to active project to reduce bandwidth`. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: RouteAnnouncer may not announce page title on navigation
issues.description: RouteAnnouncer is imported and rendered but if it does not read the current route's page title and announce it via aria-live, screen reader users and the accessibility audit will flag missing navigation announcements. This is a Global Acceptance Standard violation.
issues.prompt: In the file src/App.tsx, make these specific changes: Verify that the RouteAnnouncer component is rendered inside the Router (HashRouter) context so it can access useLocation(). BEFORE: RouteAnnouncer may be rendered outside the router context or may not have access to route metadata for meaningful announcements. AFTER: Ensure <RouteAnnouncer /> is placed inside <HashRouter> but outside the <Routes> block. It should use useLocation() to detect route changes and announce the page name. If the component already does this correctly, no change needed — just verify its placement is inside the router. If it is currently outside <HashRouter>, move it inside. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: QueryClient defaults lack staleTime and error retry configuration
issues.description: The queryClient is imported from a separate file but based on patterns, it likely uses TanStack Query defaults (staleTime: 0, retry: 3). For construction PM with frequently viewed but rarely changing data (budget, schedule), a 30-second staleTime would reduce unnecessary refetches. The retry count should be 2 for mutations to avoid duplicate creates.
issues.prompt: In the file src/lib/queryClient.ts, make these specific changes: BEFORE: The QueryClient is likely created with default options (staleTime: 0 meaning every component mount triggers a refetch, and retry: 3 for all query types). AFTER: Configure the QueryClient with construction-appropriate defaults: `new QueryClient({ defaultOptions: { queries: { staleTime: 30_000, gcTime: 300_000, retry: 2, refetchOnWindowFocus: false }, mutations: { retry: 1 } } })`. The staleTime of 30 seconds means data fetched in the last 30 seconds is served from cache without a network request — critical for field users on slow connections. refetchOnWindowFocus: false prevents jarring refetches when a super tabs back to the app. Mutation retry of 1 prevents duplicate creates. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

competitive_intel: Procore's infrastructure includes robust chunk-loading recovery, global error boundaries with branded fallback pages, and service worker caching for field reliability. Fieldwire excels at offline-first architecture with IndexedDB-backed routing that never shows a blank screen. SiteSync's infrastructure layer has the right imports (ErrorBoundary, OfflineBanner, ServiceWorker hooks, Realtime subscriptions) but lacks the polish to handle degraded conditions gracefully — the exact conditions superintendents face daily on construction sites.
summary: The Infrastructure & Routing module has a solid architectural skeleton — lazy loading, error boundaries, realtime hooks, offline awareness, auth protection, and API versioning are all present as imports and wiring. However, the execution has critical gaps: the error boundary has no visible fallback UI (crashes produce blank screens), Suspense lacks ARIA announcements (accessibility failure), ProtectedRoute likely doesn't preserve returnTo URLs (broken deep linking), and lazy imports have no retry logic (fatal on flaky jobsite networks). The API v1 router file appears duplicated with a truncated function. The five most impactful fixes target: (1) ErrorBoundary fallback UI, (2) Suspense accessibility, (3) ProtectedRoute returnTo preservation, (4) lazy import retry for field reliability, and (5) router.ts cleanup. These are all surgical one-file fixes that directly address the Global Acceptance Standards and PAGE_ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA for auth flow, loading states, and error states.
